title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) generate large amounts summary statistics require effective visualization interpretation publication. standard tools R (qqman, CMplot) rely base R graphics offer limited customization. Modern genomics workflows benefit plotting system integrates ggplot2 — enabling layered customization, journal-specific theming, seamless composition multi-panel figures. ggwas designed three goals: Comprehensive coverage — 17 plot types one package, including novel visualizations available elsewhere. Publication-ready defaults — sensible colors, proper axis formatting, built-journal themes plots look good without tweaking. Performance scale — smart downsampling handles 10M+ variant datasets without manual intervention. package reads output PLINK, REGENIE, GCTA, GEMMA, generic tab-separated file. plot functions return standard ggplot objects, can add layers, override themes, combine panels patchwork exactly ggplot.

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
code: # From GitHub pak::pak("bczech/ggwas")  # Once available on Bioconductor: # BiocManager::install("ggwas")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: fastest way load GWAS results read_gwas_table(), auto-detects common column naming conventions (CHR/CHROM/#CHROM, BP/POS/GENPOS, P/PVALUE/LOG10P, etc.): result gwas_data object — data.frame validated columns custom print method showing key QC metrics glance. specific tools, dedicated readers handle format quirks automatically: can also pass data.frame directly — just specify non-standard column names:
code: library(ggwas) #> ggwas v0.99.2 library(ggplot2)  data(example_gwas) example_gwas #> A gwas_data object: 8,000 variants across 22 chromosomes #>   Min p-value: 1.19e-14 #>   Lambda GC:   3.996 #>   Columns:     CHR, BP, SNP, P, BETA, SE, A1, A2, AF gwas <- read_gwas_table("results.txt")       # auto-detect gwas <- read_plink_assoc("results.assoc")    # PLINK gwas <- read_plink_linear("results.assoc.linear") gwas <- read_regenie("results.regenie")      # REGENIE (handles LOG10P) gwas <- read_gcta_mlma("results.mlma")       # GCTA gwas <- read_gemma("results.assoc.txt")      # GEMMA manhattan_plot(my_df, chr = "chrom", bp = "position", p = "pval")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Manhattan plot workhorse GWAS visualization — point tested variant, plotted genomic position (x) association strength -log10(p-value) (y). Chromosomes alternate color visual separation. interpret: Look “towers” points rising genome-wide significance line (red dashed, p = 5×10⁻⁸). tower locus evidence true association. Isolated points line worth investigating may artifacts. broad signal spanning multiple chromosomes equally (rather discrete peaks) can indicate unresolved population stratification — check QQ plot case.  Label strongest signals switch different palette:
code: manhattan_plot(example_gwas) manhattan_plot(   example_gwas,   label_top_n = 5,   colors = gwas_palette("vibrant") )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: loci extremely low p-values (e.g., 1e-50), compress rest plot. y_truncate breaks y-axis — region two values cut , break symbol (//) marks transition:  single value auto-detects resume point data:
code: # Add extreme p-values for demonstration gwas_extreme <- example_gwas gwas_extreme$P[1:2] <- c(1e-50, 1e-30)  # Cut the region between 15 and 45 on the -log10(p) scale manhattan_plot(gwas_extreme, y_truncate = c(15, 45), label_top_n = 3) manhattan_plot(gwas_extreme, y_truncate = 15, label_top_n = 3)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: genome-wide suggestive significance lines fully customizable. default 5e-8 assumes standard human GWAS ~1M independent tests, correct threshold depends number variants, correction method, organism:
code: # Bonferroni for 500k SNPs manhattan_plot(example_gwas, genome_wide = 0.05 / 500000, suggestive = 1e-4) # No threshold lines manhattan_plot(example_gwas, genome_wide = NULL, suggestive = NULL)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Manhattan plot can look different alternative palettes themes — useful matching journal style guides:
code: manhattan_plot(example_gwas, colors = gwas_palette("lancet")) + theme_science() manhattan_plot(example_gwas, colors = gwas_palette("nejm")) + theme_cell()

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: quantile-quantile plot compares observed p-value distribution expectation null hypothesis. interpret: Points follow diagonal closely lower left (bulk non-associated variants) deviate upward tail (true signals). points lift diagonal early uniformly, indicates genomic inflation — either population stratification cryptic relatedness sample. grey band 95% confidence interval null: points inside consistent association. λ_GC > 1.05 well-powered GWAS polygenic signal expected; λ_GC > 1.2 without clear polygenic explanation warrants investigation.  Stratifying minor allele frequency helps diagnose whether inflation driven rare variants (common imputed data) uniform:
code: qq_plot(example_gwas, show_lambda = TRUE, ci = 0.95) example_gwas$MAF_bin <- cut(   example_gwas$AF,   breaks = c(0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5),   labels = c("Rare (<5%)", "Low (5-20%)", "Common (>20%)") ) qq_plot(example_gwas, group = "MAF_bin")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Miami plot mirrors two Manhattan plots vertically — one pointing , one pointing . standard way compare discovery replication cohorts, two related traits, shared genomic coordinate axis. interpret: Peaks genomic position panels replicate across cohorts. Peaks present one panel may cohort-specific (sample composition, ancestry differences) underpowered study. Use discovery/replication comparison two related phenotypes (e.g., BMI vs waist-hip ratio) see shared loci:
code: miami_plot(   example_gwas, example_gwas,   top_title = "Discovery",   bottom_title = "Replication" )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: identify significant region, locus plot shows fine structure association within locus. interpret: lead SNP (lowest p-value) anchors plot. LD data provided, points colored r² lead — red points (high LD) also reach significance support association signal, blue points (low LD) reach significance independently may represent secondary signal locus. gene track underneath shows genes fall within region, guiding biological follow-:
code: locus_plot(example_gwas, region_chr = 1, region_start = 1e6, region_end = 50e6)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: compact representation association signals across entire genome. x-axis binned genomic position, y-axis chromosome, color intensity reflects strongest signal bin. use: works well supplementary overview figure — shows genome-wide patterns Manhattan plots can obscure overplotting. Hot spots (bright tiles) across multiple chromosomes suggest polygenicity; single intense cluster points one major locus. also handles 10M+ variant datasets without downsampling pre-aggregates.  Different palettes emphasize different signal ranges:
code: pvalue_heatmap(example_gwas, bin_size = 10e6, palette = "magma") pvalue_heatmap(example_gwas, bin_size = 10e6, palette = "viridis") pvalue_heatmap(example_gwas, bin_size = 10e6, palette = "inferno")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Unlike RNA-seq volcano plot (uses fold change), plots GWAS effect size (β̂\hat{\beta}) significance. see signals protective vs risk-increasing, large effects relative statistical evidence. Points can colored significance status chromosome:  Color chromosome instead significance:
code: volcano_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 5) volcano_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 5, color_by = "chromosome")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: circular (circos-style) Manhattan arranges chromosomes around ring. layout compact works particularly well multi-trait comparisons trait occupies ring:
code: circular_manhattan(example_gwas, colors = gwas_palette("nature"))

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: need single figure shows full picture — Manhattan, QQ, top hits table, p-value distribution — gwas_summary() assembles multi-panel layout automatic tags (, B, C, D) suitable supplementary figures:
code: gwas_summary(example_gwas)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: manuscripts, rs IDs less informative gene names. manhattan_genes() takes gene annotation table labels lead SNP nearest gene. practice, extract table GTF file via biomaRt — define small set manually:  peaks close together, arrow-style annotations avoid label overlap:
code: genes <- data.frame(   chr = c(1, 2, 5, 7, 11, 15, 19, 22),   start = c(2e6, 50e6, 80e6, 30e6, 60e6, 40e6, 10e6, 20e6),   end = c(3e6, 52e6, 85e6, 35e6, 65e6, 45e6, 15e6, 25e6),   gene = c("MTOR", "NRXN1", "TCF7L2", "CDKAL1", "KCNQ1", "FTO", "LDLR", "COMT") )  manhattan_genes(example_gwas, genes = genes, gene_p_threshold = 0.001) manhattan_genes(   example_gwas, genes = genes,   gene_p_threshold = 0.001,   arrow = TRUE, label_face = "italic" )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: top_hits() extracts independent lead SNPs using window-based clumping (1 Mb default), annotates estimated cytoband, optionally maps nearest gene. result plain data.frame ready export manuscript table:

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Mark specific genomic regions colored band — example MHC (chr6:25-34 Mb), known risk loci prior GWAS, candidate regions linkage studies:
code: plt <- manhattan_plot(example_gwas)  regions <- data.frame(   chr = c(6, 1),   start = c(25e6, 5e6),   end = c(35e6, 30e6),   label = c("MHC", "1p36") )  highlight_regions(plt, regions, color = "#FFD700", alpha = 0.2)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Pleiotropy — one variant influencing multiple traits — pervasive complex genetics. Visualizing requires overlaying GWAS results multiple traits shared genomic axis. interpret: Diamond-shaped highlighted points variants reaching genome-wide significance one trait simultaneously. Peaks appear multiple colors position suggest shared genetic mechanisms, worth following formal colocalization Mendelian randomization:
code: set.seed(123) trait2 <- example_gwas trait2$P <- runif(nrow(trait2))^3 trait2$P[sample(nrow(trait2), 10)] <- 10^(-runif(10, 5, 10))  multitrait_manhattan(   BMI = example_gwas,   Height = trait2,   colors = "nature",   highlight_shared = TRUE )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: GWAS hits fall non-coding regions, making functional interpretation challenging. Overlaying genomic annotations (enhancers, promoters, eQTLs, open chromatin ENCODE/Roadmap) helps determine whether signals enrich regulatory elements relevant tissue interest. interpret: Colored points fall within annotated functional regions; grey background points . significant peaks preferentially overlap one annotation type (e.g., liver enhancers lipid traits), provides evidence regulatory mechanism tissue action:
code: annotations <- data.frame(   chr = c(1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 19),   start = c(5e6, 20e6, 50e6, 30e6, 60e6, 40e6, 10e6),   end = c(30e6, 50e6, 120e6, 80e6, 100e6, 80e6, 40e6),   category = c("Enhancer", "Gene", "eQTL", "Gene",                "Enhancer", "eQTL", "Gene") )  enrichment_manhattan(example_gwas, annotations = annotations, palette = "nature")

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: multi-ring layout stacks traits concentrically — ring independent GWAS, sharing angular coordinate system:
code: circular_manhattan(   list(BMI = example_gwas, Height = trait2),   colors = "nature", point_size = 0.6 )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Phenome-wide association studies test one variant hundreds thousands phenotypes — common biobank-era research (UK Biobank, FinnGen, Us). interpret: point phenotype. Phenotypes grouped domain (metabolic, immune, cardiovascular, etc.) along x-axis. Triangles pointing indicate risk-increasing effects; pointing , protective. cluster significant associations within one domain suggests variant influences biological pathway rather single trait. Isolated significant hits across multiple domains may indicate pleiotropy shared confounder (e.g., BMI-mediated effects):
code: phewas_data <- data.frame(   phenotype = paste0("Pheno_", 1:60),   p = 10^(-runif(60, 0, 10)),   category = rep(c("Metabolic", "Immune", "Neurological",                     "Cardiovascular", "Musculoskeletal", "Other"), 10),   beta = rnorm(60, 0, 0.3) )  phewas_plot(phewas_data, label_top_n = 5)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Colocalization analysis asks: GWAS signal eQTL (two GWAS traits) share causal variant, driven different variants happen nearby? interpret: two panels share genomic coordinate axis. peak top panel (GWAS) aligns peak bottom panel (eQTL), signals likely share causal variant — gene regulated eQTL strong candidate GWAS hit. Offset peaks suggest distinct causal variants LD. visual check complements formal colocalization methods (coloc, eCAVIAR):
code: set.seed(42) eqtl_data <- example_gwas eqtl_data$P <- runif(nrow(eqtl_data))^2  coloc_plot(   example_gwas, eqtl_data,   region_chr = 1, region_start = 1e6, region_end = 30e6,   top_title = "GWAS", bottom_title = "eQTL" )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: identifying GWAS locus, fine-mapping narrows variant likely causal. Tools like SuSiE FINEMAP assign variant posterior inclusion probability (PIP, 0–1) group high-PIP variants credible sets. interpret: Large points high PIP — strong causal candidates. Color indicates credible set membership: variants set LD statistically distinguished. locus one large red point (PIP > 0.8) well-resolved; locus many medium-sized points one set residual uncertainty. Multiple credible sets one locus suggest independent signals:
code: fm_data <- example_gwas fm_data$PIP <- runif(nrow(fm_data))^5 fm_data$PIP[which.min(fm_data$P)] <- 0.92 fm_data$PIP[order(fm_data$P)[2:3]] <- c(0.65, 0.4) fm_data$credible_set <- NA_integer_ fm_data$credible_set[fm_data$PIP > 0.05] <- 1L  finemapping_plot(   fm_data,   region_chr = 1, region_start = 1e6, region_end = 50e6,   label_pip_above = 0.3 )

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Genetic correlation (rg) measures much genetic basis two traits overlaps. ranges -1 (perfectly opposing genetic effects) 0 (independent) +1 (shared genetic architecture). LDSC GREML estimate rg GWAS summary statistics alone, without individual-level data. interpret: Red cells indicate traits share risk variants (e.g., BMI T2D). Blue cells indicate opposing genetics (e.g., height T2D). Clustering reveals groups genetically related traits. Stars mark statistically significant correlations. figure essential understanding trait relationships cross-trait genomics:  Alternative palette without clustering:
code: traits <- c("BMI", "Height", "WHR", "T2D", "CAD", "SBP", "HDL") set.seed(123) rg <- matrix(0, 7, 7, dimnames = list(traits, traits)) rg[lower.tri(rg)] <- runif(21, -0.6, 0.8) rg <- rg + t(rg) diag(rg) <- 1  genetic_correlation(rg, show_values = TRUE, palette = "RdBu") genetic_correlation(rg, show_values = TRUE, palette = "PRGn", cluster = FALSE)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: relationship minor allele frequency effect size reveals trait’s genetic architecture. interpret: complex traits show cloud small effects common frequencies (lower left) — polygenicity. Rare variants large effects (upper left) characteristic Mendelian-like architecture. empty upper-right corner (large effects common MAF) expected natural selection removes variants. see points , check coding errors population-stratification artifacts:
code: architecture_plot(example_gwas, p_threshold = 0.001, label_top_n = 5)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Visualize genotyped imputed variants distributed across genome. Regions low coverage (centromeres, heterochromatin) appear gaps, high-density regions stand . Two rendering styles available. Heatmap style bins variants colors tiles count — best large datasets:  Points style draws individual variants chromosome outlines — density visible natural clustering:  Points can colored local density, association significance, uniformly:  palette argument works styles:
code: snp_density(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6, chr_info = chr_info_human()) snp_density(example_gwas, style = "points", chr_info = chr_info_human()) snp_density(example_gwas, style = "points",   color_by = "significance", chr_info = chr_info_human()) snp_density(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6,   palette = "magma", chr_info = chr_info_human())

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Built-chromosome data available human, mouse, cattle. species, use chr_info_ucsc() fetch data UCSC:
code: # Built-in (instant, offline) snp_density(gwas, chr_info = chr_info_human())   # hg38 snp_density(gwas, chr_info = chr_info_mouse())   # mm39 snp_density(gwas, chr_info = chr_info_cattle())  # ARS-UCD1.2  # Any species from UCSC (requires internet) snp_density(gwas, chr_info = chr_info_ucsc("canFam6"))   # dog snp_density(gwas, chr_info = chr_info_ucsc("susScr11"))  # pig

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: critical quality-control visualization: compare genotyping density (top track) association signal strength (bottom track) chromosome. helps distinguish genuine signals density artifacts — region shows strong association high variant density, signal driven uneven coverage rather biology.  Regions bottom track lights top track uniform convincing signals.
code: density_signal_plot(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6)

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: ggwas ships 14 palettes covering different use cases — colorblind-safe defaults, journal-inspired schemes, RColorBrewer integrations: palette can passed directly plot functions via colors argument. Use gwas_palette(name, n = k) interpolate need specific number colors (e.g., multi-trait plots).
code: gwas_palettes() #>  [1] "default"       "colorblind"    "vibrant"       "pastel"        #>  [5] "dark"          "nature"        "science"       "lancet"        #>  [9] "nejm"          "aaas"          "brewer_set1"   "brewer_set2"   #> [13] "brewer_paired" "brewer_dark2" gwas_palette("nature", n = 5) #> [1] "#E64B35" "#4DBBD5" "#00A087" "#3C5488" "#F39B7F"

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: journal style requirements figure fonts, sizes, axis formatting. Built-themes handle don’t need manually adjust parameters submission:  Available themes: theme_nature(), theme_science(), theme_cell(), theme_plos(), theme_presentation(), theme_poster().
code: manhattan_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 3) + theme_nature()

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: preset bundles together theme, color palette, appropriate point sizes given context. fastest way switch exploratory analysis publication-ready output:  Available presets: "publication", "presentation", "poster", "exploratory".
code: p <- gwas_preset("publication") manhattan_plot(example_gwas, colors = p$colors, point_size = p$point_size) + p$theme

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: large datasets (common imputed GWAS 10-40M variants), ggwas applies smart downsampling default. algorithm preserves significant near-significant variants spatially binning dense cloud non-significant points. resulting plot visually indistinguishable plotting points, renders seconds rather minutes:
code: manhattan_plot(large_gwas)                        # auto (target: 200k points) manhattan_plot(large_gwas, downsample = FALSE)    # all points (slow) manhattan_plot(large_gwas, downsample_n = 500000) # custom target

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Since every ggwas function returns standard ggplot object, can compose multi-panel figures using patchwork way ggplot:
code: library(patchwork)  p1 <- manhattan_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 3) +   labs(tag = "A") + theme_nature() p2 <- qq_plot(example_gwas) +   labs(tag = "B") + theme_nature() p3 <- volcano_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 5) +   labs(tag = "C") + theme_nature()  p1 / (p2 | p3) + plot_layout(heights = c(2, 1))

title: ggwas: Modern GWAS Visualizations
text: Standard ggplot2 export dimensions matching journal requirements:
code: fig <- manhattan_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 5) + theme_nature()  # Nature — single column (89 mm width) ggsave("figure1.pdf", fig, width = 89, height = 55, units = "mm")  # Full-width figure (183 mm) ggsave("figure1_wide.pdf", fig, width = 183, height = 80, units = "mm")  # High-res PNG for presentations ggsave("figure1.png", fig, width = 10, height = 5, dpi = 300)

title: Annotate peaks with nearest gene names — annotate_genes
text: data gwas_data object data.frame. genes data.frame gene positions. Required columns: chr (integer "chr1" format), start, end, gene (gene symbol). Optional: strand. p_threshold annotate SNPs p < value. max_distance Maximum distance (bp) SNP gene midpoint assign annotation. title: Annotate peaks with nearest gene names — annotate_genes

title: Annotate peaks with nearest gene names — annotate_genes
code: data(example_gwas) genes <- data.frame(   chr = c(1, 2, 5), start = c(1e6, 50e6, 80e6),   end = c(1.5e6, 51e6, 82e6), gene = c("GENE_A", "GENE_B", "GENE_C") ) annotated <- annotate_genes(example_gwas, genes, top_n = 10) head(annotated[!is.na(annotated$nearest_gene), ]) #> Warning: no non-missing arguments to min; returning Inf #> A gwas_data object: 0 variants across 0 chromosome #>   Min p-value: Inf #>   Lambda GC:   NA #>   Columns:     CHR, BP, SNP, P, BETA, SE, A1, A2, AF, nearest_gene, gene_distance

title: Genetic architecture plot — architecture_plot
text: Visualize relationship minor allele frequency effect size, revealing whether trait's genetic architecture polygenic (many small effects) oligogenic (large effects). Points colored significance.

title: Genetic architecture plot — architecture_plot
code: architecture_plot(   data,   beta = NULL,   p = NULL,   af = NULL,   p_threshold = 5e-08,   colors = c(significant = "#E64B35", nonsignificant = "#BDC3C7"),   point_size = 1,   alpha = 0.5,   log_maf = FALSE,   show_density = FALSE,   label_top_n = NULL,   title = NULL )

title: Genetic architecture plot — architecture_plot
text: data gwas_data object data.frame P, BETA, AF columns. beta, p, af Column name overrides. p_threshold P-value threshold coloring significant variants. colors Named vector "significant" "nonsignificant" colors. point_size Point size. alpha Transparency. log_maf TRUE, use log10(MAF) x-axis. show_density TRUE, add marginal density curves. label_top_n Label top N variants significance. title Plot title.

title: Genetic architecture plot — architecture_plot
text: ggplot object.

title: Genetic architecture plot — architecture_plot
code: data(example_gwas)  # Basic architecture plot architecture_plot(example_gwas)   # Label top hits, lower threshold for demo architecture_plot(example_gwas, p_threshold = 0.001, label_top_n = 5)   # Log-scale MAF axis architecture_plot(example_gwas, p_threshold = 0.001, log_maf = TRUE)

code: coloc_plot(   gwas1,   gwas2,   chr = NULL,   bp = NULL,   p = NULL,   snp = NULL,   region_chr = NULL,   region_start = NULL,   region_end = NULL,   lead_snp = NULL,   flank = 5e+05,   ld = NULL,   ld_colors = c("#2166AC", "#67A9CF", "#78C679", "#F4A582", "#D73027"),   top_title = "Trait 1",   bottom_title = "Trait 2",   highlight_snps = NULL,   point_size = 2,   title = NULL )

title: Colocalization locus plot — coloc_plot
text: gwas1 First dataset (e.g., GWAS). gwas_data data.frame. gwas2 Second dataset (e.g., eQTL). format. chr, bp, p, snp Column name overrides (applied datasets). region_chr Chromosome region plot. region_start, region_end Start end positions. lead_snp SNP ID center (± flank). flank Flank size bp around lead_snp. ld Named numeric vector LD r² values (optional). ld_colors Colors LD bins. top_title Label top panel. bottom_title Label bottom panel. highlight_snps SNPs highlight panels. point_size Point size. title Overall title.

title: Density–signal dual-track plot — density_signal_plot
text: Compare SNP genotyping density association signal strength across genome. chromosome two rows: top track shows number variants per bin (density), bottom track shows minimum p-value per bin (signal). helps distinguish genuine association signals artifacts driven uneven genotyping imputation coverage.

title: Density–signal dual-track plot — density_signal_plot
code: density_signal_plot(   data,   chr = NULL,   bp = NULL,   p = NULL,   bin_size = 1e+06,   chr_info = NULL,   density_palette = "viridis",   signal_palette = "magma",   chromosomes = NULL,   title = NULL )

title: Density–signal dual-track plot — density_signal_plot
text: data gwas_data object data.frame GWAS results. chr, bp, p Column name overrides. bin_size Bin size base pairs (default 1 Mb). chr_info Optional data.frame chr length columns. NULL, chromosome lengths inferred data. density_palette Color palette density track. signal_palette Color palette signal track. chromosomes Optional integer vector chromosomes display. title Plot title.

title: Density–signal dual-track plot — density_signal_plot
text: ggplot object.

title: Density–signal dual-track plot — density_signal_plot
code: data(example_gwas, package = "ggwas") density_signal_plot(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6)   # With chromosome info for accurate lengths density_signal_plot(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6,   chr_info = chr_info_human())   # Subset to specific chromosomes density_signal_plot(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6, chromosomes = 1:5)   # Different palettes density_signal_plot(example_gwas, bin_size = 5e6,   density_palette = "plasma", signal_palette = "inferno")

Variants set get color. chr, bp, p, snp Column name overrides. region_chr, region_start, region_end Region plot. lead_snp Center SNP ± flank. flank Flank size bp. pip_min_size Minimum point size (PIP ≈ 0). pip_max_size Maximum point size (PIP ≈ 1). set_colors Colors credible sets. nonsig_color Color variants credible set. show_pip_legend Show PIP size legend. label_pip_above Label variants PIP value. title Plot title.

title: Fine-mapping credible set plot — finemapping_plot
text: ggplot object.

title: Fine-mapping credible set plot — finemapping_plot
code: data(example_gwas) # Add simulated fine-mapping results example_gwas$PIP <- runif(nrow(example_gwas))^4 example_gwas$PIP[which.min(example_gwas$P)] <- 0.95 example_gwas$credible_set <- NA example_gwas$credible_set[example_gwas$PIP > 0.1] <- 1L finemapping_plot(example_gwas, region_chr = 1,                  region_start = 1e6, region_end = 50e6)

what: ggwas: Modern 'ggplot2' Visualizations for Genome-Wide Association Studies — ggwas-package
title: ggwas: Modern 'ggplot2' Visualizations for Genome-Wide Association Studies — ggwas-package
text: Create publication-ready visualizations genome-wide association studies. Provides 17 plot types including Manhattan, QQ, Miami, locus zoom, PheWAS, colocalization, fine-mapping, genetic correlation, SNP density, density-signal comparison plots. Reads PLINK, REGENIE, GCTA, GEMMA formats natively. plot functions return 'ggplot2' objects full customization. Smart downsampling handles datasets 10 million+ variants efficiently.

title: ggwas: Modern 'ggplot2' Visualizations for Genome-Wide Association Studies — ggwas-package

title: Manhattan plot — manhattan_plot
text: Create genome-wide Manhattan plot GWAS summary statistics. Returns ggplot2 object customization.

title: Manhattan plot — manhattan_plot
code: manhattan_plot(   data,   chr = NULL,   bp = NULL,   p = NULL,   snp = NULL,   colors = c("#1A5276", "#76D7C4"),   point_size = 0.8,   alpha = 1,   genome_wide = 5e-08,   suggestive = 1e-05,   threshold_colors = c("#E74C3C", "#3498DB"),   highlight_snps = NULL,   highlight_color = "#E74C3C",   highlight_size = 2,   label_snps = NULL,   label_top_n = NULL,   label_column = "SNP",   downsample = TRUE,   downsample_n = 2e+05,   chromosomes = NULL,   chr_labels = NULL,   y_limit = NULL,   y_truncate = NULL,   title = NULL )

title: Manhattan plot — manhattan_plot
text: data gwas_data object data.frame GWAS results. chr, bp, p, snp Column name overrides (auto-detected NULL). colors Two-color vector alternating chromosomes. point_size Point size. alpha Point transparency. genome_wide Genome-wide significance threshold (p-value). suggestive Suggestive significance threshold. threshold_colors Colors significance lines. highlight_snps Character vector SNP IDs highlight. highlight_color Color highlighted SNPs. highlight_size Size highlighted points. label_snps Character vector SNP IDs label. label_top_n Label top N significant SNPs. label_column Column use label text. downsample Enable smart downsampling large datasets. downsample_n Target number points downsampling. chromosomes Subset chromosomes plot (integer vector). chr_labels Custom chromosome labels (character vector, length displayed chromosomes). NULL, auto-generated chromosome numbers. y_limit Upper y-axis limit -log10(p). y_truncate Break y-axis cut middle region. Either single value (break point, resumes max value) vector two values c(break_from, resume_at) defining cut range -log10(p) units. example, y_truncate = c(15, 50) shows 0-15 full scale, cuts 15-50, shows 50+ break. title Plot title.

title: Manhattan plot — manhattan_plot
text: ggplot object.

title: Manhattan plot — manhattan_plot
code: data(example_gwas, package = "ggwas")  # Basic Manhattan plot manhattan_plot(example_gwas)   # Label top hits with a different palette manhattan_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 5, colors = gwas_palette("vibrant"))   # Nature journal style manhattan_plot(example_gwas, label_top_n = 3) + theme_nature()   # Lancet palette with Science theme manhattan_plot(example_gwas, colors = gwas_palette("lancet")) + theme_science()   # Subset chromosomes manhattan_plot(example_gwas, chromosomes = 1:10)   # NEJM palette manhattan_plot(example_gwas, colors = gwas_palette("nejm"), label_top_n = 3)   # Broken y-axis: cut 10-50, show 0-10 and 50+ manhattan_plot(example_gwas, y_truncate = c(10, 50))   # Single value: auto-detect resume point manhattan_plot(example_gwas, y_truncate = 10)   # Custom significance threshold (Bonferroni for 500k SNPs) manhattan_plot(example_gwas, genome_wide = 0.05 / 500000)   # No threshold lines manhattan_plot(example_gwas, genome_wide = NULL, suggestive = NULL)

title: SNP density karyogram — snp_density
text: data gwas_data object data.frame GWAS results. chr, bp, p Column name overrides. style Rendering style: "heatmap" binned density tiles, "points" individual variant positions chromosome outlines. bin_size Bin size base pairs (default 1 Mb). used style = "heatmap". color_by style = "points": color dots. "density" (default) colors local density, "significance" colors -log10(p), "uniform" single color. chr_info Optional data.frame columns chr, length, optionally centromere_start, centromere_end. Use chr_info_human() hg38. NULL, chromosome lengths inferred data. palette Color palette: "viridis", "magma", "inferno", "plasma". point_size Point size style = "points". point_alpha Point transparency style = "points". show_centromeres TRUE centromere positions available chr_info, draw centromere markers. downsample_n Maximum number points plot "points" style. title: GWAS effect-size volcano plot — volcano_plot
text: Plot effect size (BETA) statistical significance (-log10(p)), revealing magnitude direction genetic effects. Unlike RNA-seq volcano plots, variant can color chromosome scale point size allele frequency.

title: GWAS effect-size volcano plot — volcano_plot
code: volcano_plot(   data,   beta = NULL,   p = NULL,   snp = NULL,   p_threshold = 5e-08,   beta_threshold = NULL,   color_by = "significance",   size_by = NULL,   point_size = 0.8,   alpha = 0.6,   label_snps = NULL,   label_top_n = NULL,   colors = c(significant = "#E74C3C", nonsignificant = "#BDC3C7"),   title = NULL )

title: GWAS effect-size volcano plot — volcano_plot
text: data gwas_data object data.frame. beta, p, snp Column name overrides. p_threshold P-value significance threshold. beta_threshold Minimum |BETA| consider significant. color_by color points: "significance", "chromosome", column name. size_by Column name scale point size (e.g. "AF"), NULL. point_size Base point size (used size_by NULL). alpha Point transparency. label_snps Character vector SNP IDs label. label_top_n Label top N significant SNPs. colors Named color vector: "significant" "nonsignificant" significance mode, custom palette. title Plot title.
